stem: A 16-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Sudden severe pain in my left testicle that woke me up."Vital SignsBP 138/82HR 102RR 18Temp 98.6°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Testicular Torsion is the most likely diagnosis. Classic presentation: sudden severe pain, high-riding testicle with horizontal lie, absent cremasteric reflex, adolescent male.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Waiting for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high - go to OR
- Assuming epididymitis in an adolescent (torsion is more common in this age group)
- Not recognizing intermittent torsion (pain that resolved spontaneously)
- Forgetting that cremasteric reflex ABSENT = highly suggestive of torsion

Evidence: Testicular Torsion - StatPearls - Sharp VJ, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK448199. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Testicular Torsion: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Salvage rate: 24hrs = <10%. Time is testicle!
- Torsion of Appendix Testis: Can mimic torsion but is benign. 'Blue dot sign' at upper pole is pathognomonic. Managed conservatively but if uncertain, explore.
- Epididymitis: Less common in adolescents than adults. Usually more gradual onset. Cremasteric reflex preserved. Positive Prehn sign (relief with elevation).
- Orchitis: Often associated with mumps or epididymitis. More gradual onset.

Reference: Testicular Torsion - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448199/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 72-year-old female presents to the emergency department, stating: "My mother is confused and acting strange."Vital SignsBP 128/76HR 78RR 16Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 96% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH) is the most likely diagnosis. Euvolemic, concentrated urine, high urine sodium, low serum osmolality. Classic pattern.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Correcting sodium too fast (>8-10 mEq/L in 24h) → osmotic demyelination syndrome
- Not determining the volume status (hypovolemic vs euvolemic vs hypervolemic)
- Giving normal saline when the problem is SIADH (can worsen hyponatremia!)
- Not checking urine studies (urine Na, urine osmolality)

Evidence: Hyponatremia - StatPearls - Hoorn EJ, Zietse R. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK470386. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Underlying Malignancy: SIADH is commonly paraneoplastic - small cell lung cancer is classic. Must image chest.
- CNS Disease: CNS lesions (tumor, bleed, infection, trauma) can cause SIADH. Consider head imaging.
- Drug-Induced SIADH: Many drugs cause SIADH (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide). Review medications.
- Hypothyroidism: Can cause hyponatremia. Check TSH. Usually less severe.

Reference: Hyponatremia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470386/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 34-year-old transgender female presents to the clinic, stating: "Severe pain in my right side that came on suddenly."Vital SignsBP 148/92HR 98RR 18Temp 98.8°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stone) is the most likely diagnosis. Classic presentation: sudden severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin with hematuria. Note: Patient has male anatomy despite identifying as female.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Assuming all flank pain is kidney stones without considering AAA in appropriate patients
- Missing fever which suggests infected stone requiring urgent intervention
- Not checking for hydronephrosis which may require urgent urology consultation
- Forgetting to strain urine to capture stone for analysis

Evidence: AUA/Endourology Society Kidney Stones Guidelines 2022 - Assimos D, et al. J Urol. 2022;207(1):8-15

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Pyelonephritis: Must consider infection, especially if fever present. Infected obstructing stone is urologic emergency.
- Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm: Always consider in older patients or those with risk factors. Can mimic renal colic.
- Appendicitis: Right-sided pain but typically periumbilical migrating to RLQ, not flank to groin.
- Musculoskeletal Pain: MSK pain is positional, not colicky, and doesn't radiate to groin.

Reference: AUA/Endourology Society Kidney Stones Guidelines 2022 - https://www.auanet.org/guidelines [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Fatigue and swelling for 2 months."Vital SignsBP 148/94HR 78RR 16Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 98% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: CKD Stage 4 is the most likely diagnosis. GFR 22 - must find cause.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Attributing to diabetes (he has none)
- Not asking about occupation and heat exposure
- Missing that HTN is secondary, not cause

- Mesoamerican Nephropathy/CKDu: Young, no DM, no HTN, minimal proteinuria, agricultural work.

stem: A 24-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "Burning with urination for 2 days."Vital SignsBP 118/74HR 72RR 14Temp 98.8°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Vaginitis is the most likely diagnosis. Can cause external dysuria - ask about discharge, odor.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Not distinguishing internal vs external dysuria (UTI vs vaginitis)
- Missing STI - always ask about sexual history
- Treating without culture in complicated patients
- Forgetting pregnancy test in reproductive-age women

- Pyelonephritis: Must assess for fever, flank pain to rule out upper tract infection.
- Urethritis (STI): Sexually active - must consider chlamydia/gonorrhea.
- Interstitial Cystitis: Chronic symptoms, negative cultures - not this acute presentation.

stem: A 72-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "I can't urinate and my belly hurts terribly."Vital SignsBP 168/94HR 92RR 18Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 97% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute Urinary Retention (BPH) is the most likely diagnosis. Elderly male with longstanding LUTS, enlarged prostate, unable to void. Classic BPH-related retention.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Delaying catheterization while waiting for workup
- Not monitoring for post-obstructive diuresis after catheter placement
- Removing catheter too soon without alpha-blocker trial
- Missing medication causes (antihistamines, decongestants, anticholinergics)

Evidence: AUA/SUFU Guideline on the Management of BPH 2021 - Lerner LB, et al. J Urol. 2021;206(4):806-826

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Obstructive Uropathy/AKI: Prolonged retention can cause bilateral hydronephrosis and renal failure. Check creatinine.
- Prostate Cancer: Can present similarly to BPH. Need PSA and possible biopsy after acute episode resolves.
- UTI: Can precipitate retention but not primary cause. Usually has dysuria, frequency, urgency before retention.
- Bladder Stone: Can cause intermittent obstruction. Usually has positional symptoms, hematuria.

Reference: AUA/SUFU Guideline on the Management of BPH 2021 - https://www.auanet.org/guidelines/benign-prostatic-hyperplasia-(bph)-guideline [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 34-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "Worst pain of my life in my side that goes to my groin."Vital SignsBP 158/96HR 102RR 18Temp 99.1°FSpO₂ 98% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Ureteral Stone (Nephrolithiasis) is the most likely diagnosis. Classic presentation: sudden colicky flank pain radiating to groin, microscopic hematuria, young male.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Not ruling out AAA in older patients with flank pain
- Missing infected obstructed kidney (fever + stone = emergency)
- Over-imaging with CT in young patients with classic presentation and hematuria
- Not providing adequate analgesia - renal colic is extremely painful

Evidence: AUA/Endourology Society Guideline on Kidney Stones 2014 (Amended 2021) - Assimos D, et al. J Urol. 2016;196(4):1153-1160

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Obstructed Infected Kidney: Stone + fever + pyuria = EMERGENCY. Requires emergent decompression.
- AAA (in older patients): Always consider in patients >50 with flank pain, especially with vascular risk factors.
- Musculoskeletal Pain: Would be constant, reproduced with movement/palpation. Not colicky.
- Appendicitis (if right-sided): Right flank pain can mimic appendicitis. CT will differentiate.

Reference: AUA/Endourology Society Guideline on Kidney Stones 2014 (Amended 2021) - https://www.auanet.org/guidelines/kidney-stones-guideline [Accessed 2025]
Reference: Renal-gu - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "I haven't been feeling right for a few days - really tired and I've noticed I'm not urinating as much."Vital SignsBP 98/62HR 96RR 18Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 96% on room air This 68-year-old man with CKD Stage 3 (baseline Cr 1.6) on lisinopril presents with oliguria, fatigue, and confusion after 10 days of high-dose ibuprofen for gout. He has had decreased oral intake with vomiting. Labs show Cr 5.2, BUN 78, K 5.8. What is the most likely mechanism of his AKI?

choices.text: Acute interstitial nephritis from ibuprofen allergy
Prerenal azotemia from volume depletion alone

explanation:
Correct Answer: Correct! This is the classic 'triple whammy': 1) ACE inhibitor (blocks angiotensin-mediated efferent arteriolar constriction), 2) NSAID (blocks prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation), 3) Volume depletion (reduced renal perfusion). Combined, these severely reduce GFR in vulnerable CKD kidneys.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- While he has BPH, there's no palpable bladder and he has multiple risk factors for prerenal/intrinsic AKI. Obstruction doesn't explain the timeline with NSAID use.
- Volume depletion is a major contributor, but pure prerenal AKI doesn't fully explain the severity in the context of NSAIDs + ACE inhibitor. This is likely a combination.
- AIN is possible with NSAIDs but typically presents with fever, rash, and eosinophilia. He lacks these features. The hemodynamic mechanism is more likely given the clinical picture.

Evidence: KDIGO AKI Guidelines 2012 - Kidney Int Suppl 2012;2:1-138

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Post-renal obstruction from BPH: While he has BPH, there's no palpable bladder and he has multiple risk factors for prerenal/intrinsic AKI. Obstruction doesn't explain the timeline with NSAID use.
- Prerenal azotemia from volume depletion alone: Volume depletion is a major contributor, but pure prerenal AKI doesn't fully explain the severity in the context of NSAIDs + ACE inhibitor. This is likely a combination.
- Acute interstitial nephritis from ibuprofen allergy: AIN is possible with NSAIDs but typically presents with fever, rash, and eosinophilia. He lacks these features. The hemodynamic mechanism is more likely given the clinical picture.

Reference: KDIGO AKI Guidelines 2012 - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441896/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 72-year-old man with HTN on lisinopril and HCTZ presents with 3 days of diarrhea and decreased oral intake. He is orthostatic. Cr has risen from baseline 1.1 to 2.8 mg/dL.Vital SignsBP 98/62 supine, 72/50 standingHR 92 supine, 118 standingLabsBUN 58Cr 2.8 (baseline 1.1)BUN/Cr 20.7Urine Na 8 mEq/LFeNa 0.4%Urine Osm 650 mOsm/kgSediment Bland (no casts) What type of AKI is this, and how do the urine indices confirm the mechanism?

choices.text: PRERENAL AKI — the kidney is RESPONDING APPROPRIATELY to hypovolemia

explanation:
Correct Answer: AKI by urine indices: PRERENAL (kidney responding to hypoperfusion): FeNa 20, Urine Osm >500, bland sediment. INTRINSIC (ATN): FeNa >2%, Urine Na >40, BUN/Cr <15, Urine Osm <350, muddy brown casts. Caveat: FeNa unreliable on diuretics — use FEUrea (<35% = prerenal).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Diarrhea + decreased intake + orthostasis = volume depletion
- FeNa 0.4% = prerenal
- BUN/Cr 20.7 = disproportionate urea rise
- Bland sediment = tubules intact
- ACEi + diuretic + volume depletion = classic prerenal

Evidence: AKI - StatPearls - Rahman M, et al. StatPearls. NBK441896. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- ATN: ATN has FeNa >2%, muddy brown casts. This patient has FeNa 0.4% and bland sediment.
- Postrenal: Obstruction shows hydronephrosis on US. Clinical picture is volume depletion.
- Contrast nephropathy: No contrast exposure mentioned.
- AIN: AIN has WBC casts, eosinophiluria. Sediment is bland here.

Reference: AKI - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441896/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old woman with small cell lung cancer has sodium 124 mEq/L. She is euvolemic.LabsNa 124Serum Osm 258 (low)Urine Na 65 (>40)Urine Osm 520 (>100)TSH 2.1AM cortisol 18 (normal)Uric acid 2.1 (low) What is the stepwise diagnostic approach and diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Hyponatremia algorithm: Step 1: Serum Osm (confirm hypotonic). Step 2: Volume status. Step 3: Urine Osm/Na. SIADH criteria: euvolemic + hypotonic + Uosm >100 + UNa >40 + normal thyroid/adrenal.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Serum Osm 258 = hypotonic
- Euvolemic = not CHF/cirrhosis
- Urine Osm 520 = inappropriately concentrated
- SCLC = classic SIADH cause

Evidence: SIADH - StatPearls - Verbalis JG, et al. StatPearls. NBK507777. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- CHF: Euvolemic, no edema.
- Polydipsia: Would have dilute urine (Osm <100).
- Adrenal insufficiency: Cortisol is normal.
- CSW: CSW is hypovolemic.

Reference: SIADH - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK507777/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old man with CKD stage 4 and DM on lisinopril presents with weakness. ECG shows peaked T waves, widened QRS, and loss of P waves.Vital SignsBP 142/88HR 52LabsK 7.8 mEq/LCr 4.2BUN 68Glucose 142 What is the treatment sequence for severe hyperkalemia with ECG changes?

choices.text: Kayexalate alone will resolve the hyperkalemia
Severe hyperkalemia with ECG changes — treatment sequence:

explanation:
Correct Answer: Hyperkalemia treatment by mechanism:
• Stabilize membrane: Calcium (immediate, doesn't lower K)
• Shift K into cells: Insulin + glucose, albuterol, bicarbonate
• Eliminate K: Diuretics, kayexalate/patiromer, dialysis

ECG changes in hyperkalemia (progressive): peaked T waves (5.5-6.5) → PR prolongation → QRS widening → loss of P waves → sine wave → VF/asystole. Any ECG changes = give calcium immediately. Insulin dose: always give with glucose (D50 25-50g) to prevent hypoglycemia; check glucose at 1 and 2 hours.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- K 7.8 with ECG changes = cardiac emergency
- Peaked T + widened QRS + loss of P = severe
- Calcium first to stabilize membrane
- Insulin + D50 to shift K into cells
- Consider dialysis given CKD stage 4
- Hold ACEi (lisinopril contributing)

Evidence: Hyperkalemia - StatPearls - Palmer BF, et al. StatPearls. NBK470284. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Kayexalate alone: Kayexalate takes 1-2 hours for effect and does not address immediate cardiac toxicity. Calcium must be given first when ECG changes are present.
- Insulin without glucose: Insulin without glucose causes hypoglycemia in 10-75% of patients. Always give D50 with insulin; monitor glucose at 1 and 2 hours.
- Dialysis always required: Dialysis is for refractory cases or severe CKD. Many patients respond to medical management. This patient may need dialysis given CKD4, but calcium and insulin come first.
- Calcium lowers K: Calcium does NOT lower potassium. It stabilizes the cardiac membrane, reducing the risk of arrhythmia. Insulin, albuterol, and dialysis actually lower K.

Reference: Hyperkalemia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 42-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive bilateral leg swelling and periorbital puffiness worse in the morning. He also reports frothy urine. He has no history of heart or liver disease. He has type 2 diabetes with HbA1c of 9.2%.Vital SignsBP 148/92HR 76ExamExtremities 3+ pitting edema bilateral, extending to thighsFace Periorbital edema (worse in morning)Cardiac No JVD, no S3, lungs clearAbdomen No ascites, no hepatomegalyLabsAlbumin 1.8 g/dL (very low, normal 3.5-5.0)Cr 1.4Cholesterol 342 mg/dL (elevated)Triglycerides 268 mg/dL (elevated)Urinalysis 4+ protein, oval fat bodies, maltese crosses24h urine protein 6.2 g/day (nephrotic range >3.5g/day) What is the syndrome and the workup to determine the cause?

choices.text: Heart failure causing edema — start diuretics
NEPHROTIC SYNDROME — defined by the clinical tetrad:
Cirrhosis causing edema and low albumin
Minimal change disease given the clinical picture

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT: Edema differential by mechanism

| Feature | Nephrotic | Heart Failure | Cirrhosis |
|---------|-----------|--------------|----------|
| Edema pattern | Periorbital + LE | LE + dependent | Ascites + LE |
| JVD/S3 | Absent | Present | Absent |
| Proteinuria | >3.5g/day | Mild (Nephrotic syndrome causes by age:
• Children: minimal change disease (most common)
• Adults: diabetic nephropathy, FSGS, membranous
• Elderly: membranous, amyloidosis

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Bilateral edema + periorbital puffiness = nephrotic distribution
- Frothy urine = proteinuria
- No JVD, no S3, lungs clear = NOT heart failure
- No ascites, no hepatomegaly = NOT cirrhosis
- 24h protein 6.2g = nephrotic range (>3.5g)
- Albumin 1.8 = severe hypoalbuminemia
- Cholesterol 342, TG 268 = hyperlipidemia
- Oval fat bodies = lipiduria (nephrotic marker)
- Diabetes with HbA1c 9.2 = diabetic nephropathy likely
- Workup: serologies to exclude other causes

Evidence: Nephrotic Syndrome - StatPearls - Hull RP, et al. StatPearls. NBK470444. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Heart failure: Heart failure would have JVD, S3, orthopnea, pulmonary rales, and elevated BNP. This patient has NONE of these cardiac findings. His edema is from nephrotic syndrome — massive proteinuria causing hypoalbuminemia and decreased oncotic pressure. The frothy urine and nephrotic-range proteinuria confirm the diagnosis.
- Cirrhosis: Cirrhosis causes low albumin and edema, but presents with ascites (often before peripheral edema), spider angiomata, caput medusae, palmar erythema, and coagulopathy. This patient has NO liver stigmata, no ascites, and has MASSIVE proteinuria — the low albumin is from urinary losses, not decreased liver synthesis.
- Protein-losing enteropathy: PLE can cause hypoalbuminemia but is associated with diarrhea, GI symptoms, and does NOT cause proteinuria. This patient has 6.2g/day proteinuria and oval fat bodies in urine — the protein is clearly being lost through the kidneys, not the GI tract.
- Minimal change disease: MCD is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in CHILDREN, but in a 42-year-old diabetic man with HbA1c 9.2%, DIABETIC NEPHROPATHY is far more likely. MCD in adults would need to be confirmed by renal biopsy showing podocyte foot process effacement. The diabetic history makes workup for diabetic nephropathy the priority.

Reference: Nephrotic Syndrome - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470444/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 68yo man CKD4, weakness. K+ 6.8. ECG: peaked T waves, widened QRS.Vital SignsBP 148/88HR 52RR 18Temp 98.4FSpO₂ 96% Most appropriate immediate next step?

choices.text: IV calcium gluconate, then insulin/glucose, emergent dialysis

explanation:
Correct Answer: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes: (1) IV calcium gluconate (cardiac stabilization), (2) insulin+glucose (shift K+), (3) emergent dialysis.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Calcium stabilizes membrane in 1-3 min, does NOT lower K+
- Insulin 10U + D50 shifts K+ in 15-30 min
- Albuterol adjunctive but insufficient alone
- Definitive: dialysis, kayexalate, patiromer

Evidence: Hyperkalemia - StatPearls - Simon LV, et al. NBK470284. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Kayexalate: Too slow for emergency.
- Albuterol only: Insufficient alone.
- NS bolus: Doesn't address hyperK.
- Furosemide: Too slow, may not work in CKD.

Reference: Hyperkalemia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 55yo woman CKD3b (GFR 38), UPCR 1.2g/g despite max lisinopril. BP 138/86.Vital SignsBP 138/86HR 76RR 14Temp 98.4FSpO₂ 99% Next step to slow CKD progression?
explanation:
Correct Answer: SGLT2 inhibitors reduce CKD progression independent of diabetes (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY). Standard of care with ACEi/ARB for proteinuric CKD.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- DAPA-CKD: 39% reduction in progression
- Benefits independent of diabetes
- Mechanism: reduce intraglomerular pressure
- Complements ACEi/ARB

Evidence: SGLT2i in CKD - StatPearls - Heerspink HJL, et al. NBK588452. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- HCTZ: BP but doesn't slow progression.
- Switch to ARB: Equivalent to ACEi.
- EPO: For anemia, not progression.
- Dialysis: Premature at GFR 38.

Reference: SGLT2i in CKD - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK588452/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 48yo man, nephrotic syndrome (8g/day proteinuria, albumin 1.8), persistent edema despite furosemide 80mg BID.Vital SignsBP 128/78HR 82RR 18Temp 98.4FSpO₂ 97% Next step for diuretic resistance?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Diuretic resistance: sequential nephron blockade - add metolazone 30 min before furosemide to block compensatory distal Na reabsorption.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Distal nephron hypertrophy compensates
- Metolazone + furosemide: synergistic
- Give metolazone 30 min before furosemide
- Monitor K/Mg closely

Evidence: Nephrotic Syndrome - StatPearls - Kodner C. NBK470444. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Increase furosemide: Ceiling dose; diminishing returns.
- Switch HCTZ: Weaker than furosemide; add as adjunct instead.
- IV albumin alone: Transient effect; best combined with furosemide.
- Fluid restriction: Adjunct but insufficient alone.

Reference: Nephrotic Syndrome - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470444/ [Accessed 2025]
